content.assessment: Delaying selection to age 14 would require substantial legislative amendment to Act CXC's statutory framework establishing 6- and 8-year gimnázium entry points, and critically, it cannot be imposed unilaterally on church-maintained and private institutions, which operate outside the Klebelsberg Centre's direct governance authority. This structural reform has the highest legal-authority burden of all proposals, since it eliminates entry points rather than merely reforming admissions procedures within them.
opportunities: A negotiated settlement with maintainers, if achieved, could produce a durable and comprehensive legal resolution rather than a partial one
strengths: Directly resolves the centralized-exam-versus-free-choice tension by eliminating the early entry points where the exam gate applies [F-live-legal_and_governance-02]
threats: Non-participation by church/private maintainers could produce a two-tier system where public schools comply but private/church schools retain early selection, undermining the reform's stated equity goal
weaknesses: Requires primary legislative change to a core statutory framework, the most legally demanding intervention among the proposals [F-live-legal_and_governance-01]

Cannot be enforced against church-maintained and private maintainers without separate negotiated agreement, creating a maintainer-autonomy barrier to uniform implementation [F-live-legal_and_governance-03]
